title: 10 Thai Street Food Dishes You Can Name, Point To & Order

vocab.explanation: ผัดไทย is Thailand's most famous noodle dish — thin rice noodles stir-fried with egg, bean sprouts, crushed peanuts, and your choice of protein, finished with a squeeze of lime. ผัด means 'to stir-fry' and ไทย means 'Thai', so it literally means 'Thai stir-fry'. You'll find it at almost every street food stall, and it's one of the safest dishes to start with. Just say the name and point — vendors know exactly what you mean.

exampleSentences.thai: ผัดไทยอร่อยมากครับ
exampleSentences.note: A simple compliment you can give the vendor after eating — it will make their day.

vocab.thai: ข้าวมันไก่
vocab.thaiSplit: ข้าว มัน ไก่
vocab.explanation: ข้าวมันไก่ is poached chicken served over rice cooked in rich chicken broth, with a ginger-garlic dipping sauce on the side. ข้าว means 'rice', มัน means 'fatty' or 'oily', and ไก่ means 'chicken'. It's one of the most beloved everyday Thai meals — simple, filling, and done to perfection at dedicated stalls that have been perfecting their recipe for decades. You'll see it everywhere from morning markets to late-night spots.

exampleSentences.thai: ข้าวมันไก่หนึ่งจานค่ะ
exampleSentences.translit: khâaw man gài nùeng jaan khâ
exampleSentences.english: One plate of chicken rice, please.
exampleSentences.note: The natural way to order — dish name, then quantity, then counter word. หนึ่งจาน = one plate.

vocab.explanation: ส้มตำ is shredded unripe papaya pounded in a clay mortar with chilli, garlic, lime, fish sauce, and palm sugar. You can hear vendors making it from across the market — the rhythmic pounding sound is unmistakable. ส้ม means 'sour' and ตำ means 'to pound'. A word of warning: Thai ส้มตำ can be extremely spicy. If you're not sure, ask for เผ็ดนิดหน่อย (just a little spicy) or ไม่เผ็ด (not spicy).

exampleSentences.thai: ส้มตำไม่เผ็ดได้ไหมคะ
exampleSentences.translit: sôm-tam mâi phèt dâi mǎi khá
exampleSentences.english: Can I have the papaya salad not spicy?
exampleSentences.note: ไม่เผ็ด = not spicy. This phrase will save you — Thai ส้มตำ is often much hotter than expected.

vocab.thai: ก๋วยเตี๋ยว
vocab.thaiSplit: ก๋วยเตี๋ยว
vocab.explanation: ก๋วยเตี๋ยว is the Thai word for noodle soup — any noodle soup. It covers everything from clear broth with pork slices to rich boat noodles with roasted chilli, to rice noodles, glass noodles, or egg noodles. This single word unlocks a huge category of Thai street food. At a noodle stall, you'll usually be asked your noodle type and protein. The tones on this word take a little practice — gǔuay has a rising tone, tǐaw has a falling tone. Keep saying it and it'll click.

exampleSentences.thai: ก๋วยเตี๋ยวหมูน้ำใสครับ
exampleSentences.english: Pork noodle soup with clear broth, please.
exampleSentences.note: หมู = pork, น้ำใส = clear broth. Swap หมู for ไก่ (chicken) or เนื้อ (beef) to change the protein.

vocab.explanation: หมูปิ้ง are small skewers of marinated pork grilled over charcoal — one of Thailand's great street snacks. หมู means 'pork' and ปิ้ง means 'to grill'. They're especially popular in the morning; หมูปิ้ง is actually a common Thai breakfast food, often eaten with sticky rice. You'll find vendors with glowing charcoal grills at the roadside, and they're sold by the skewer. The counter word for skewers is ไม้ — which literally means 'stick' or 'wood'.

exampleSentences.thai: ขอหมูปิ้งสามไม้ค่ะ
exampleSentences.english: Can I get three pork skewers please.
exampleSentences.note: สาม = three, ไม้ = the counter word for skewers (means 'stick').

vocab.explanation: ข้าวผัด is Thai fried rice — jasmine rice stir-fried with egg, spring onion, and your choice of meat or seafood. ข้าว is 'rice' and ผัด is 'to stir-fry'. It's one of the most beginner-friendly dishes to order: mild flavour, recognisable ingredients, and available everywhere. The best part is how easily you can customise it — just add your protein after ข้าวผัด: ข้าวผัดหมู (pork), ข้าวผัดไก่ (chicken), ข้าวผัดกุ้ง (shrimp). One word changes the whole dish.

exampleSentences.thai: ข้าวผัดหมูหนึ่งจานค่ะ
exampleSentences.translit: khâaw phàt mǔu nùeng jaan khâ
exampleSentences.english: One plate of pork fried rice, please.
exampleSentences.note: Add the protein directly after ข้าวผัด. หนึ่งจาน = one plate.

vocab.explanation: ไก่ย่าง is charcoal-grilled chicken, marinated in garlic, coriander root, and palm sugar. ไก่ means 'chicken' and ย่าง means 'to grill'. It's especially popular in northern and north-eastern Thailand, where you'll see whole chickens rotating on roadside grills and the smell is absolutely incredible. It's the classic partner to ส้มตำ and ข้าวเหนียว (sticky rice) — the north-eastern combo you'll see everywhere. Usually served with น้ำจิ้ม, a sweet chilli dipping sauce.

exampleSentences.thai: ขอไก่ย่างครึ่งตัวครับ
exampleSentences.translit: khǎaw gài yâang khrûeng tua khráp
exampleSentences.english: Can I get half a grilled chicken please.
exampleSentences.note: ครึ่งตัว = half a bird. ครึ่ง = half, ตัว = the counter word for animals.

vocab.english: I'd like / may I have (polite request)
vocab.explanation: ขอ is one of the single most useful words in Thai. It means 'I'd like' or 'may I have' — it's the polite, natural way to order or request anything. Put ขอ at the beginning, then what you want: ขอผัดไทย (I'd like Pad Thai), ขอน้ำ (can I have some water), ขอบิล (can I have the bill). Thai culture values warmth and politeness very highly, and starting your order with ขอ instead of just naming the dish makes you sound natural and respectful. Vendors will notice, and they'll appreciate it.

exampleSentences.thai: ขอน้ำเปล่าหนึ่งขวดค่ะ
exampleSentences.translit: khǎaw náam plào nùeng khùuat khâ
exampleSentences.english: Can I have one bottle of plain water please.
exampleSentences.note: น้ำเปล่า = plain water, หนึ่งขวด = one bottle. ขอ + item + quantity is the core ordering pattern for everything.

blocks.speakerNote: A = lost tourist at a street food stall, trying to order by pointing. B = friendly street food vendor behind the grill. Visual cue: A wears a light backpack and slightly confused expression; B wears an apron and stands behind a counter prop or table. Voice modulation: A speaks slower, more hesitant Thai; B speaks naturally and warmly. Recording note: This block is the opening clip — record it as a continuous take that can be lifted as a 30-second short. Nine plays both characters in split-frame; record A side first, then B side, then edit together in post.

lines.english: A street food stall. A tourist points at everything. The vendor is patient.
lines.speaker: narrator

lines.thai: ขอ... อันนี้ครับ
lines.english: I'd like... this one please.
lines.speaker: A

lines.thai: ผัดไทยไหมคะ?
lines.english: Pad Thai?
lines.speaker: B

lines.thai: ใช่! ผัดไทยครับ!
lines.english: Yes! Pad Thai!
lines.speaker: A

lines.thai: ใส่กุ้งไหมคะ?
lines.english: Would you like shrimp?
lines.speaker: B

lines.thai: อ้า... ใส่เลยครับ!
lines.english: Uh... yes, add it!
lines.speaker: A

lines.thai: แล้วก็ขอหมูปิ้งด้วยครับ
lines.translit: láaew gâaw khǎaw mǔu-pîng dûuay khráp
lines.english: And I'd also like pork skewers.
lines.speaker: A

lines.thai: เผ็ดไหมคะ?
lines.english: Spicy?
lines.speaker: B

lines.thai: ไม่เผ็ดครับ
lines.english: Not spicy please.
lines.speaker: A

lines.thai: ได้เลยค่ะ รอแป๊บนึงนะคะ
lines.translit: dâi looei khâ, raw pâaep nùeng ná khâ
lines.english: Of course, just a moment.
lines.speaker: B

lines.thai: ไม่ยากเลยนะครับ!
lines.english: That wasn't hard at all!
lines.speaker: A

blocks.speakerNote: Cultural context alternatives for Nine to choose from during recording: (1) STATISTIC OPENER — 'Thailand has an estimated 500,000 street food vendors — more per capita than almost anywhere in the world. In many Thai cities, eating street food is cheaper and faster than cooking at home, so a huge number of people eat almost every meal outside. Knowing what to order isn't just useful — it's how you actually eat in Thailand.' (2) PATTERN OPENER — 'Did you notice in that sketch? The tourist only used three things: ขอ to start the order, the dish name, and ไม่เผ็ด to control the spice. That's basically the whole system. By the end of this video, you'll have eight dish names and the ordering pattern to go with them.' (3) COUNTERINTUITIVE OPENER — 'Most people think ordering Thai food is complicated because the names look hard. But street food vendors hear the same ten dishes hundreds of times a day. Even a rough attempt at the Thai name will land. The goal isn't perfect pronunciation — it's enough Thai that the vendor lights up.' Default in lines below is the statistic opener.

lines.english: That tourist in the sketch? She just ordered Pad Thai and pork skewers using three words and a hand gesture. By the end of this video, you'll be able to do the same thing — and name eight of Thailand's most iconic street food dishes while you're at it.

Here's something that might surprise you. Thailand has an estimated 500,000 street food vendors. In many Thai cities — Bangkok especially, but all over the country — a huge number of people eat almost every meal outside. A lot of apartments don't even have a full kitchen, because buying street food is cheaper and faster than cooking at home.

That means street food isn't a tourist activity in Thailand. It's just... life. And knowing even a handful of dish names will make a huge difference — not just for eating, but for connecting with people. Vendors are some of the friendliest people you'll meet, and even a basic attempt at Thai gets you a big smile.
By the end of this video, you'll be able to name eight classic Thai street food dishes, use the key ordering word ขอ, and put together a real order at a street stall — all in Thai.
Let's start with the vocabulary. I'm going to go through each word one by one. For each one, I'll say the Thai, explain what it means, and show you how to use it in a sentence. Ready? Let's go.

lines.thai: ผัดไทย

lines.english: Our first dish...

ผัดไทย — phàt-thai. Thailand's most famous noodle dish, and probably the first Thai food most people ever try. Thin rice noodles, stir-fried with egg, bean sprouts, and crushed peanuts, with a squeeze of lime on the side. ผัด means 'to stir-fry', ไทย means 'Thai' — so it literally means Thai stir-fry. You'll find it everywhere. Just say the name and point — you really can't go wrong with this one.

lines.thai: ผัดไทยอร่อยมากครับ

lines.english: That means Pad Thai is really delicious. A great compliment to give the vendor after you eat — they'll love it.
OK, next dish...

lines.thai: ข้าวมันไก่

lines.english: ข้าวมันไก่ — khâaw man gài. Poached chicken on top of rice that's been cooked in chicken broth. It comes with a ginger-garlic dipping sauce on the side and usually a small bowl of clear soup. ข้าว is rice, มัน is fatty or oily, ไก่ is chicken. It's one of the most popular everyday Thai meals — simple, comforting, and often served at dedicated stalls where that's all they make. They've been doing it for decades and it shows.

lines.thai: ข้าวมันไก่หนึ่งจานค่ะ
lines.translit: khâaw man gài nùeng jaan khâ

lines.english: One plate of chicken rice, please. ข้าวมันไก่ — then one plate. That's all you need to say.
This next one you'll hear before you see it...

lines.thai: ส้มตำ

lines.english: ส้มตำ — sôm-tam. Green papaya salad. Shredded unripe papaya, pounded in a clay mortar with chilli, garlic, lime, fish sauce, and palm sugar. You can literally hear vendors making it — the rhythmic pounding echoes through the whole market. ส้ม means sour, ตำ means to pound. One important warning for beginners: Thai ส้มตำ can be seriously spicy. If you need to dial it back, you can say ไม่เผ็ด for not spicy, or เผ็ดนิดหน่อย for just a little spicy. Remember those phrases.

lines.thai: ส้มตำไม่เผ็ดได้ไหมคะ
lines.translit: sôm-tam mâi phèt dâi mǎi khá

lines.english: Can I have the papaya salad not spicy? That phrase will save you more than once.
This next word unlocks a whole category of Thai food...

lines.thai: ก๋วยเตี๋ยว

lines.english: ก๋วยเตี๋ยว — gǔuay-tǐaw. Noodle soup. Any noodle soup. This is a single word that covers a huge range of Thai dishes — clear broth with pork, rich boat noodles, rice noodles, glass noodles, egg noodles. When you sit down at a noodle stall, the vendor will ask what type of noodles you want and what protein. This one word gets you in the door for all of it. The tones take a bit of practice — gǔuay goes rising, tǐaw goes falling. Keep saying it and it'll click.

lines.thai: ก๋วยเตี๋ยวหมูน้ำใสครับ

lines.english: Pork noodle soup with clear broth, please. หมู is pork, น้ำใส is clear broth. Swap in ไก่ for chicken or เนื้อ for beef — same pattern.
This next one is my personal favourite street snack...

lines.thai: หมูปิ้ง

lines.english: หมูปิ้ง — mǔu pîng. Grilled pork skewers. Small pieces of marinated pork on bamboo sticks, grilled over charcoal. หมู is pork, ปิ้ง means to grill. The marinade is usually garlic, coriander, palm sugar, and coconut milk — the smell coming off the charcoal is incredible. In Thailand, หมูปิ้ง is actually a popular breakfast food — you'll see vendors with glowing grills early in the morning. They're sold by the skewer, so the counter word you need is ไม้, which literally means stick.

lines.thai: ขอหมูปิ้งสามไม้ค่ะ

lines.english: Can I get three pork skewers please. สาม is three, ไม้ is the counter word — means stick.
OK, next dish — a very safe choice for beginners...

lines.thai: ข้าวผัด

lines.english: ข้าวผัด — khâaw phàt. Fried rice. ข้าว is rice, ผัด is to stir-fry. It's one of the most beginner-friendly dishes on this list — mild flavour, familiar ingredients, and you can customise it really easily. Just add the protein after ข้าวผัด: ข้าวผัดหมู is pork fried rice, ข้าวผัดไก่ is chicken fried rice, ข้าวผัดกุ้ง is shrimp fried rice. One word swap and you've got a completely different dish. Very flexible, very reliable.

lines.thai: ข้าวผัดหมูหนึ่งจานค่ะ
lines.translit: khâaw phàt mǔu nùeng jaan khâ

lines.english: One plate of pork fried rice, please. ข้าวผัด, then your protein, then หนึ่งจาน for one plate.
This next one is everywhere in northern and north-eastern Thailand...

lines.thai: ไก่ย่าง

lines.english: ไก่ย่าง — gài yâang. Grilled chicken. ไก่ is chicken, ย่าง means to grill. The chicken is marinated in garlic, coriander root, and palm sugar before going on the charcoal — the smell is amazing. You'll see whole chickens rotating on roadside grills at street stalls, especially up north. The classic combination is ไก่ย่าง with ส้มตำ and ข้าวเหนียว — sticky rice — and it's served with น้ำจิ้ม, a sweet chilli dipping sauce. One of the great Thai food experiences.

lines.thai: ขอไก่ย่างครึ่งตัวครับ
lines.translit: khǎaw gài yâang khrûeng tua khráp

lines.english: Can I get half a grilled chicken please. ครึ่งตัว — ครึ่ง is half, ตัว is the counter word for animals.
And the last word — probably the most important one on this whole list...

lines.thai: ขอ

lines.english: ขอ — khǎaw. This means 'I'd like' or 'may I have'. It's the polite Thai way to order or request anything. You put it at the beginning, then what you want: ขอผัดไทย — I'd like Pad Thai. ขอน้ำ — can I have some water. ขอบิล — can I have the bill. Thai culture values warmth and politeness, and starting your order with ขอ instead of just blurting out the dish name makes you sound natural and respectful. Vendors notice this. It's a small word that does a lot of work.

lines.thai: ขอน้ำเปล่าหนึ่งขวดค่ะ
lines.translit: khǎaw náam plào nùeng khùuat khâ

lines.english: Can I have one bottle of plain water please. น้ำเปล่า is plain water, หนึ่งขวด is one bottle. ขอ plus what you want — that's the formula for everything.

OK, so now you know all eight phrases. What I'm going to do now is put them together into a short conversation — imagine you're ordering at a street food stall in Thailand. I'm going to speak at natural speed, the way a Thai person would actually talk. Don't pause the video. Just listen and see how much you can understand. Ready? Here we go.

lines.thai: ขอผัดไทยหนึ่งที่ค่ะ
ใส่กุ้งไหมคะ
ใส่กุ้งด้วยนะคะ
แล้วก็ขอหมูปิ้งสองไม้ด้วยค่ะ
เผ็ดไหมคะ
เผ็ดนิดหน่อยนะคะ
รอสักครู่นะคะ

lines.english: How was that? Could you catch some of those phrases? Don't worry if you didn't get everything — that's completely normal. Now let's break it down.
I'm going to go through that same conversation again, but this time sentence by sentence. I'll say the Thai, and then I'll explain exactly what each part means.

lines.thai: ขอผัดไทยหนึ่งที่ค่ะ

lines.english: I'd like one Pad Thai, please.

ขอผัดไทยหนึ่งที่ค่ะ. ขอ is our ordering word — I'd like, may I have. ผัดไทย is Pad Thai. หนึ่ง is one. ที่ is a counter word for servings of food — think of it like saying one portion. ค่ะ is the polite female particle at the end of a sentence. The structure is: ขอ plus dish name plus quantity plus counter word plus polite particle. That pattern works for almost any food order in Thai — once you have it, you can order anything.

lines.thai: ใส่กุ้งไหมคะ

lines.english: Would you like shrimp?

ใส่กุ้งไหมคะ. ใส่ means 'to put in' or 'to add'. กุ้ง is shrimp. ไหม is the Thai yes-or-no question particle — it turns any statement into a question. คะ is the polite female particle used specifically at the end of questions. So word by word it's 'add shrimp question?' In English we flip word order to make a question. In Thai you don't change the word order at all — you just add ไหม at the end. This is a really important pattern across the whole language.

lines.thai: ใส่กุ้งด้วยนะคะ

lines.english: Yes, with shrimp please.

ใส่กุ้งด้วยนะคะ. ใส่กุ้ง again — add shrimp. ด้วย means 'too' or 'also' — it's confirming the addition. นะ is a softening particle that adds warmth and friendliness to the phrase, like 'yes please, go ahead'. ค่ะ is the polite female particle for statements. Notice that the customer echoes part of the vendor's question back — ใส่กุ้ง — and adds ด้วย to confirm. This echo-and-confirm pattern sounds very natural in Thai conversation.

lines.thai: แล้วก็ขอหมูปิ้งสองไม้ด้วยค่ะ
lines.translit: láaew gâaw khǎaw mǔu-pîng sǎawng mái dûuay khâ
láaew gâaw khǎaw mǔu-pîng sǎawng mái dûuay khâ

lines.english: And I'd also like two pork skewers, please.

แล้วก็ขอหมูปิ้งสองไม้ด้วยค่ะ. แล้วก็ means 'and then' or 'and also' — it's a linking phrase you use when adding more to an order. ขอ is our ordering word again. หมูปิ้ง is pork skewers. สอง is two. ไม้ is the counter word for skewers — it means stick. ด้วย means too. The ordering pattern is exactly the same as the first line: ขอ plus dish name plus quantity plus counter word. Once you learn this pattern once, you can order anything on the menu.

lines.thai: เผ็ดไหมคะ

lines.english: Spicy?

เผ็ดไหมคะ. เผ็ด means spicy — one of the most important words in Thai food culture. ไหม is the question particle again. คะ is the polite female question particle. Literally it's 'spicy question?' — do you want it spicy? You will hear this at almost every street food stall. Thai food can be seriously spicy by default, so this is your moment to speak up. Don't let it pass without answering.

lines.thai: เผ็ดนิดหน่อยนะคะ

lines.english: Just a little spicy, please.

เผ็ดนิดหน่อยนะคะ. เผ็ด is spicy. นิดหน่อย means 'a little bit' — นิด and หน่อย both mean 'a little', so putting them together emphasises smallness, like 'just a tiny bit'. นะ softens it and adds warmth. ค่ะ is the polite particle. You can use นิดหน่อย for all kinds of things beyond spice — not just food. It's a really versatile phrase for expressing small amounts of anything.

lines.thai: รอสักครู่นะคะ

lines.english: Just a moment please.

รอสักครู่นะคะ. รอ means 'to wait'. สัก adds a sense of 'just a little' — it softens the waiting. ครู่ means 'a moment' or 'a little while'. นะ and ค่ะ are the warmth and politeness particles again. Together it's 'please wait just a moment'. You'll hear this from vendors all the time. When you do — just smile and nod. Your food is on the way.

Now it's your turn to practise. I'm going to say each sentence slowly, and I want you to repeat after me. Don't be shy — say it out loud! Speaking is how you learn. I'll leave a gap after each sentence for you to repeat.

lines.thai: ขอผัดไทยหนึ่งที่ค่ะ
lines.thaiSplit: ขอ ผัดไทย หนึ่ง ที่ ค่ะ

lines.thai: ใส่กุ้งไหมคะ
lines.thaiSplit: ใส่ กุ้ง ไหม คะ

lines.thai: ใส่กุ้งด้วยนะคะ
lines.thaiSplit: ใส่ กุ้ง ด้วย นะ คะ

lines.thai: แล้วก็ขอหมูปิ้งสองไม้ด้วยค่ะ
lines.thaiSplit: แล้วก็ ขอ หมูปิ้ง สอง ไม้ ด้วย ค่ะ
lines.translit: láaew gâaw khǎaw mǔu-pîng sǎawng mái dûuay khâ

lines.thai: เผ็ดไหมคะ
lines.thaiSplit: เผ็ด ไหม คะ

lines.thai: เผ็ดนิดหน่อยนะคะ
lines.thaiSplit: เผ็ด นิดหน่อย นะ คะ

lines.thai: รอสักครู่นะคะ
lines.thaiSplit: รอ สัก ครู่ นะ คะ

lines.english: Great job! If that felt hard, that's totally fine — rewind and do it again. Repetition is how you get better.
OK, quiz time! I'm going to give you a situation in English, and you need to say the Thai phrase. I'll give you a few seconds to think, and then I'll give you the answer. Ready?

blocks.speakerNote: Look to camera. Pause for 3 seconds after the cue line before cutting to answer.

lines.english: You're at a street food stall and want to order one plate of fried rice. How do you say that?
Try saying it now...

lines.thai: ขอข้าวผัดหนึ่งจานค่ะ

lines.english: That's right — ขอข้าวผัด means I'd like fried rice. หนึ่งจาน is one plate. ขอ plus the dish plus the quantity — same pattern every time.

blocks.speakerNote: Pause for 3 seconds after the cue line.

lines.english: A vendor asks if you want it spicy. You want just a little spice. What do you say?
Try saying it now...

lines.thai: เผ็ดนิดหน่อยนะคะ

lines.english: That's right — เผ็ดนิดหน่อย means just a little spicy. นิดหน่อย is your go-to phrase for a little bit of anything.

blocks.speakerNote: Pause for 3 seconds after the cue line.

lines.english: You want three pork skewers. How do you order them?
Try saying it now...

lines.thai: ขอหมูปิ้งสามไม้ค่ะ

lines.english: That's right — ขอหมูปิ้งสามไม้ — I'd like three pork skewers. สาม is three and ไม้ is the counter word for skewers, which means stick.

blocks.speakerNote: Pause for 3 seconds after the cue line.

lines.english: You want green papaya salad but no spice at all. How do you order it?
Try saying it now...

lines.thai: ขอส้มตำไม่เผ็ดค่ะ

lines.english: That's right — ขอส้มตำไม่เผ็ด — I'd like papaya salad not spicy. ไม่เผ็ด is the phrase to reach for whenever you need to dial back the heat.
Let's do a quick recap of everything you learned today.
Thanks for watching! If this helped you, give it a thumbs up and subscribe so you don't miss the next one.

blocks.speakerNote: Pre-Rev-6 — may need retro-fit when template updates. This teaser uses a thematic phrase rather than a numbered episode reference.

lines.english: Next time, we're going to talk about something every food-lover in Thailand needs — how to taste food, react to it, and talk about flavours in Thai. Get ready to learn...

lines.thai: ลองชิมดู
